## Overall Verdict

**⚠️ REVIEW NEEDED**

No critical or high-severity issues. The codebase shows generally good hygiene — Zod validation on all inputs, Drizzle ORM throughout (no raw SQL), rate limiting on the public feedback endpoint, and auth checks present on every protected handler reviewed. Two medium-severity structural concerns and three low-severity findings are documented below.

---

## Issues Found

### MEDIUM — No middleware-level auth guard for API routes

**File:** `src/middleware.ts`

The Next.js middleware matcher explicitly excludes all `/api/*` routes:

```
matcher: ['/((?!api|_next|_vercel|studio(?:/.*)?|.*\..*).*)'],
```

The comment acknowledges this: *"Auth protection is handled at the page/layout level via server-side auth() checks."* This means every API route handler is solely responsible for its own auth enforcement — there is no middleware safety net. If any future route handler is added without an `auth()` check, it will be silently unauthenticated with no framework-level protection.

**Recommendation:** Add a lightweight middleware guard that verifies a session exists for any route under `/api/` that is not explicitly allowlisted as public (e.g., `/api/auth/*`, `/api/feedback`, `/api/search`, `/api/game/[slug]`). This is defense-in-depth and does not replace per-handler checks.

---

### MEDIUM — `@ts-nocheck` disables TypeScript in auth config

**File:** `src/auth.ts`, line 1

The entire auth configuration file is exempt from TypeScript type-checking via `// @ts-nocheck`. The comment attributes this to a next-auth v4/v5 type mismatch. Auth code is the highest-trust surface in the application — type errors here can produce subtle, hard-to-detect bugs (e.g., undefined fields silently coercing to falsy, wrong field shapes reaching the session callback).

**Recommendation:** Resolve the v5 type incompatibility (install `next-auth@beta` and update types) so type-checking can be re-enabled. In the interim, add inline `// @ts-expect-error` suppressions only on the specific lines that need them rather than blanket-disabling the entire file.

---

### LOW — Inconsistent user ID resolution in child-profile handlers

**File:** `src/app/api/child-profiles/[id]/route.ts`, lines 15–17

```tsx
function getUserId(session: any): string | null {
  return session?.user?.id ?? session?.user?.email ?? null
}
```

The base route (`child-profiles/route.ts`) uses only `session.user.id`. The `[id]` route falls back to `session.user.email` if `session.user.id` is absent. Since records are written with `user.id` as the foreign key, any PUT/DELETE issued while `user.id` is missing (and `email` is used instead) would fail to find the record. The fallback is dead code given the JWT callback always populates `user.id`, but it adds ambiguity in security-critical ownership checks.

**Recommendation:** Remove the email fallback. Both routes should use the same identity accessor: `session?.user?.id`. Add a comment if the fallback is intentional.

---

### LOW — No format validation on `gameSlug` in feedback endpoint

**File:** `src/app/api/feedback/route.ts`, line 10

```tsx
gameSlug: z.string().min(1).max(200),
```

The slug accepts any string up to 200 characters with no character-set restriction. SQL injection is not possible (Drizzle ORM uses parameterized queries), but accepting arbitrary characters unnecessarily widens the attack surface for any slug-handling logic added in the future.

**Recommendation:** Add a regex constraint: `z.string().regex(/^[a-z0-9-]+$/).min(1).max(200)`

---

### LOW — Feedback `comment` stored without sanitization

**File:** `src/app/api/feedback/route.ts`, line 11

```tsx
comment: z.string().max(1000).optional(),
```

The comment field is stored as-is. This is safe as long as it is always rendered with proper escaping. If it is ever rendered as raw HTML (admin dashboard, email template, etc.) without sanitization, it becomes a stored XSS vector.

**Recommendation:** Confirm that all render sites escape this field. If any admin view renders it as HTML, add server-side sanitization (e.g., `DOMPurify` or `sanitize-html`) before storage or at render time.

---

## What Looks Good

- **Drizzle ORM everywhere** — no raw SQL found in any reviewed file. All queries are parameterized.
- **Zod validation** on all POST/PUT inputs, including the admin endpoint inputs.
- **Rate limiting** on the public feedback endpoint (5 req / 60s per IP).
- **`timingSafeEqual`** used correctly in the Credentials provider — resistant to timing attacks on reviewer login.
- **CRON_SECRET bearer auth** on the admin backfill endpoint — correctly guards against unauthenticated invocation.
- **Ownership scoping** in child-profile queries — all reads and writes are filtered by `userId`, preventing IDOR between accounts.
- **No hardcoded secrets** found in any reviewed file — all credentials read from `process.env`.
